title: ROC Curve: True vs False Positives
concept: The ROC Curve maps True Positive against False Positive rates across thresholds, showing the tradeoff between catching threats and raising false alarms.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main trade-off when we lower the decision threshold of a classification model?

options.text: We increase recall (catch more positives) but decrease precision (generate more false alarms).
We increase precision (fewer false alarms) but decrease recall (catch fewer positives).
We increase both precision and recall, but lower overall accuracy.

exercises.explain: Correct! Lowering the threshold is like using a wider net: you catch more target items (high recall) but sweep in more garbage (low precision). Now let's explore another way to visualize this trade-off using rates.

cards.title: Rain or shine?
cards.body: Imagine a weather forecaster. If they always predict rain, they'll catch 100% of rainy days, but they'll also trigger false alarms on every sunny day. The ROC curve shows how a forecaster balances rain hits against false alarms.

cards.title: True vs False Positive Rates
cards.body: The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve plots the True Positive Rate (TPR) (recall) on the y-axis against the False Positive Rate (FPR) (fraction of negatives predicted positive) on the x-axis.

cards.title: The security guard
cards.body: Think of a security guard checking IDs. TPR is the rate of letting in authorized people. FPR is the rate of letting in unauthorized people by mistake. A perfect guard has 100% TPR and 0% FPR.

cards.title: Medical diagnostic test
cards.body: In medical screening, an ROC curve helps doctors see how changing a test's cut-off value shifts the balance between finding sick patients (TPR) and subjecting healthy patients to unneeded follow-up tests (FPR).

cards.title: Is accuracy always the goal?
cards.body: We often think high accuracy is enough. But accuracy fails on imbalanced data. The ROC curve helps us evaluate model power across all thresholds, showing if it is actually good at distinguishing the two classes.

cards.title: ROC Curve Space
cards.body: Understanding how a model behaves on the ROC plot:

left.text:
Hugs the top-left corner.
TPR is near 1.0, FPR is near 0.0.

right.text:
Follows the diagonal line.
TPR and FPR increase at the same rate.

exercises.prompt: On an ROC curve, what does the diagonal line from (0,0) to (1,1) represent?

options.text: A random guesser with no predictive power.
A perfect classifier with zero error.
A model that only predicts the negative class.
A model that only predicts the positive class.

exercises.explain: The diagonal line represents random chance. If your model's curve lies on this diagonal, it is performing no better than flipping a coin.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The False Positive Rate (FPR) measures the proportion of actual negative cases that were incorrectly flagged as positive.
exercises.explain: True! FPR is the ratio of False Positives to all actual negatives: FP / (TN + FP).

exercises.sentence: The y-axis of the ROC curve represents the True Positive Rate, which is identical to the metric known as ___.
exercises.explain: True Positive Rate (TPR) is the percentage of actual positive cases that the model correctly identified, which is the definition of recall.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following points on an ROC graph represents a perfect classifier?

options.text: The top-left corner (0.0 False Positive Rate, 1.0 True Positive Rate)
The bottom-left corner (0.0 False Positive Rate, 0.0 True Positive Rate)
The top-right corner (1.0 False Positive Rate, 1.0 True Positive Rate)

exercises.explain: The top-left corner represents 100% correct detection (TPR = 1) with 0% false alarms (FPR = 0).

exercises.prompt: You are evaluating two fraud-detection models. Model A's ROC curve sits strictly above Model B's ROC curve at all points. However, at a threshold of 0.5, Model B has a slightly higher accuracy. Which model is generally superior at distinguishing fraud?

options.text: Model A, because its higher ROC curve shows better overall capability to separate classes at any threshold.
Model B, because accuracy at 0.5 is the only metric that matters in production.
Neither, because ROC curves cannot compare different models.
Model B, because sitting below Model A means it makes fewer predictions.

exercises.explain: Model A is superior because its curve sits higher, meaning it achieves a higher True Positive Rate for any given False Positive Rate. Accuracy at 0.5 is just a single arbitrary threshold check! 🎉
